info.title: FeatureScript Standard Library - Math
info.description: Mathematical types and utilities — Vector, Matrix, Transform, units, coordinate systems

symbols.name: APPROXIMATION_SAMPLES
symbols.description: Number of sample taken on the curve to perform the approximation.

symbols.name: MAX_CONTROL_POINTS
symbols.description: Maximum number of control points to approximate the curve with.

symbols.name: MAX_DEGREE
symbols.description: Maximum degree of the curve.

symbols.name: TOLERANCE_BOUND
symbols.description: A `LengthBoundSpec` for approximation tolerance.

symbols.name: DEGREE_BOUND
symbols.description: An `IntegerBoundSpec` for curve degree.

symbols.name: curveApproximationPredicate
symbols.description: A predicate to add curve approximation parameters to a feature.

symbols.name: checkApproximationParameters
symbols.description: Checks approximation options to see if the approximation would succeed and highlights the issue if not.

symbols.name: approximateResults
symbols.signature: (context is Context, id is Id, definition is map)
symbols.description: Approximates the curves created by the feature id. This meant to be used along with `curveApproximationPredicate` and it expects `definition` to have the parameters defined in the predicate.

box.fs.name: box
box.fs.description: This module refers to 3D bounding boxes, e.g. the result of a call to `evBox3d`.This is not to be confused with the box standard type used for references.

symbols.name: Box3d
symbols.description: A three-dimensional bounding box.

subfields.name: minCorner
subfields.description: A 3D position representing the corner with the smallest x, y, and z coordinates.

subfields.name: maxCorner
subfields.description: A 3D position representing the corner with the largest x, y, and z coordinates.

symbols.name: canBeBox3d
symbols.description: Typecheck for `Box3d`

symbols.name: box3d
symbols.description: Construct a bounding box from two opposite corners.

symbols.name: box3d
symbols.description: Construct a bounding box containing all points in pointArray

symbols.name: transformBox3d
symbols.description: Return a box aligned with transformed coordinate system containing the input box

symbols.name: extendBox3d
symbols.signature: (bBox is Box3d, absoluteValue is ValueWithUnits, factor is number)
symbols.description: Return an enlarged bounding box. The box is scaled by `1 + factor` around its midpoint, and then each face is moved outward by `absoluteValue` (inward if `absoluteValue` is negative).

parameters.name: absoluteValue
parameters.description: The absolute distance to move each face of the box. The corners move `sqrt(3)` times as far.

parameters.name: factor
parameters.description: The relative amount to expand the box, with `0` leaving it unchanged.

symbols.name: box3dCenter
symbols.description: Return the center of the bounding box.

symbols.name: box3dDiagonalLength
symbols.description: Return the length of the diagonal from the `minCorner` to the `maxCorner` of the bounding box.

symbols.name: insideBox3d
symbols.description: Whether the specified point is within the bounding box.

symbols.name: box3dAllCorners
symbols.description: Returns all 8 corners of a Box3d as an array of Vectors.

symbols.name: WORLD_ORIGIN
symbols.description: Position of the world origin, equivalent to `vector(0, 0, 0) * meter`

symbols.name: X_DIRECTION
symbols.description: Direction parallel to the X axis, equivalent to `vector(1, 0, 0)`

symbols.name: Y_DIRECTION
symbols.description: Direction parallel to the Y axis, equivalent to `vector(0, 1, 0)`

symbols.name: Z_DIRECTION
symbols.description: Direction parallel to the Z axis, equivalent to `vector(0, 0, 1)`

symbols.name: WORLD_COORD_SYSTEM
symbols.description: The world coordinate system, equivalent to `coordSystem(vector(0, 0, 0) * meter, vector(1, 0, 0), vector(0, 0, 1))`

symbols.name: CoordSystem
symbols.description:
A right-handed Cartesian coordinate system. Used for converting points and geometry between different reference frames, or for creating planes and mate connectors.
The y-axis of a coordinate system is not stored, but it can be obtained by calling the yAxis function, which simply performs a cross product.
 See also: `toWorld(CoordSystem)`
`fromWorld(CoordSystem)`
`coordSystem(Plane)`
`plane(CoordSystem)`
`opMateConnector`

subfields.name: origin
subfields.description: A 3D point, in world space, representing the origin of the coordinate system.

subfields.name: xAxis
subfields.description: A 3D unit vector, in world space, representing the x-axis of the coordinate system.

subfields.name: zAxis
subfields.description: A 3D unit vector, in world space, representing the z-axis of the coordinate system. Must be perpendicular to the `xAxis`.

symbols.name: coordSystem
symbols.signature: (origin is Vector, xAxis is Vector, zAxis is Vector)
symbols.description:
Creates a Cartesian coordinate system.
 See also: `CoordSystem`

parameters.name: origin
parameters.description: A 3D point in world space.

parameters.name: xAxis
parameters.description: A 3D vector in world space. Need not be normalized.

parameters.name: zAxis
parameters.description: A 3D vector in world space. Need not be normalized but must be orthogonal to xAxis.

symbols.name: tolerantEquals
symbols.description: Check that two `CoordSystem`s are the same up to tolerance.

symbols.name: toWorld
symbols.description:
Convert a specified point from a specified coordinate system into world space. 
 Example: 
`toWorld(cSys, vector(0, 0, 0)) equals cSys.origin`

parameters.name: pointInCSys
parameters.description: A 3D vector, measured with respect to the `cSys` provided.

symbols.name: toWorld
symbols.description:
Returns a `Transform` which will transform coordinates measured in `cSys` into world coordinates. 
 Example: 
`toWorld(cSys) * vector(0, 0, 0) equals cSys.origin`When used in operations which place or move parts (like `opTransform`, `opPattern`, or `addInstance`), this transform will (somewhat counterintuitively) move parts from the world origin and orientation to the `cSys` origin and orientation.

symbols.name: fromWorld
symbols.description:
Convert a specified point from world space into a specified coordinate system. 
 Example: 
`fromWorld(cSys, cSys.origin) equals vector(0, 0, 0)`

parameters.name: worldPoint
parameters.description: A 3D vector, measured in world space.

symbols.name: fromWorld
symbols.description:
Returns a `Transform` which will transform coordinates measured in world space into `cSys` coordinates. 
 Example: 
`fromWorld(cSys) * cSys.origin equals vector(0, 0, 0)`When used in operations which place or move parts (like `opTransform`, `opPattern`, or `addInstance`), this transform will (somewhat counterintuitively) move parts from the `cSys` origin and orientation to the world origin and orientation.

symbols.name: scaleNonuniformly
symbols.signature: (xScale is number, yScale is number, zScale is number, cSys is CoordSystem)
symbols.description: Returns a `Transform` that represents 3 independent scalings along the X, Y, and Z axes of a particular `cSys`, centered around `cSys.origin`.

symbols.name: yAxis
symbols.description: Returns the y-axis of a coordinate system

symbols.name: toString
symbols.description: Returns a representation of the coordinate system as a string.

symbols.name: X_AXIS
symbols.description: The global X axis, equivalent to `line(vector(0, 0, 0) * meter, vector(1, 0, 0))`

symbols.name: Y_AXIS
symbols.description: The global Y axis, equivalent to `line(vector(0, 0, 0) * meter, vector(0, 1, 0))`

symbols.name: Z_AXIS
symbols.description: The global Z axis, equivalent to `line(vector(0, 0, 0) * meter, vector(0, 0, 1))`

symbols.name: Line
symbols.description: Represents a parameterized line in 3D space.

subfields.name: origin
subfields.description: A point on the line, as a 3D Vector with length units.

subfields.name: direction
subfields.description: A unitless normalized 3D Vector.

symbols.name: canBeLine
symbols.description: Typecheck for `Line`

symbols.name: line
symbols.description: Creates a line from a point and a direction.

parameters.name: direction
parameters.description: The direction gets normalized by this function.

symbols.name: tolerantEquals
symbols.description:
Check that two `Line`s are the same up to tolerance, including checking that they have the same origin.
To check if two `Line`s are equivalent (rather than equal), use `collinearLines`.

symbols.name: collinearLines
symbols.description: Returns `true` if the two lines are collinear.

symbols.name: transform
symbols.description: Returns the transformation that transforms the line `from` to the line `to` (including the origin) using the minimum rotation angle.

symbols.name: project
symbols.description: Returns the projection of the point onto the line. See also other overloads of `project`.

symbols.name: rotationAround
symbols.description: Returns a `Transform` that represents the rotation around the given line by the given angle. The rotation is counterclockwise looking against the line direction.

symbols.name: LineLineIntersection
symbols.description: Represents an intersection between two lines. Depending on the lines, this intersection may be a point, a line, or nothing.

subfields.name: dim
subfields.description:
Integer representing the dimension of the intersection. 
 Example: 
`0 indicates that intersection is a 3D length Vector.` Example: 
`1 indicates that intersection is a Line. (i.e. the lines are collinear)` Example: 
`-1 indicates that the intersection does not exist.`

subfields.name: intersection
subfields.description: `undefined` or `Vector` or `Line` (depending on `dim`) that represents the intersection.

symbols.name: canBeLineLineIntersection
symbols.description: Typecheck for `LineLineIntersection`

symbols.name: intersection
symbols.description: Returns a `LineLineIntersection` representing the intersection between two lines. If the lines are collinear, `line1` will be stored in the `intersection` field of that `LineLineIntersection`.

symbols.name: isPointOnLine
symbols.description: Returns true if the point lies on the line.

symbols.name: Circle
symbols.description: Represents a circle in 3D space.

subfields.name: coordSystem
subfields.description: The circle lies in the xy plane of this coordinate system and the origin of its parameterization is the x axis.

subfields.name: radius
subfields.description: The radius of the circle.

symbols.name: canBeCircle
symbols.description: Typecheck for `Circle`

symbols.name: circle
symbols.description: Returns a new `Circle` in the given coordinate system `cSys`.

symbols.name: circle
symbols.signature: (center is Vector, xDirection is Vector, normal is Vector, radius is ValueWithUnits)
symbols.description: Returns a new `Circle` with the given parameters. `xDirection` and `normal` must be perpendicular.

symbols.name: tolerantEquals
symbols.description: Check that two `Circle`s are the same up to tolerance, including the coordinate system.

symbols.name: Ellipse
symbols.description: Represents an ellipse in 3D space.

subfields.name: coordSystem
subfields.description: The ellipse lies in the xy plane of this coordinate system and the x axis corresponds to the major radius.

subfields.name: majorRadius
subfields.description: The larger of the two radii.

subfields.name: minorRadius
subfields.description: The smaller of the two radii.

symbols.name: canBeEllipse
symbols.description: Typecheck for `Ellipse`

symbols.name: ellipse
symbols.signature: (cSys is CoordSystem, majorRadius is ValueWithUnits, minorRadius is ValueWithUnits)
symbols.description: Returns a new `Ellipse` with the given parameters.

symbols.name: ellipse
symbols.signature: (center is Vector, xDirection is Vector, normal is Vector, majorRadius is ValueWithUnits, minorRadius is ValueWithUnits)
symbols.description: Returns a new `Ellipse` with the given parameters. `xDirection` and `normal` must be perpendicular.

symbols.name: tolerantEquals
symbols.description: Check that two `Ellipse`s are the same up to tolerance, including the coordinate system.

symbols.name: KnotArray
symbols.description: An array of non-decreasing numbers representing the knots of a spline

symbols.name: canBeKnotArray
symbols.description: Typecheck for `KnotArray`

symbols.name: knotArray
symbols.description: Cast an array on non-decreasing numbers to a KnotArray

symbols.name: knotArrayIsCorrectSize
symbols.signature: (knots is KnotArray, degree is number, nControlPoints is number)
symbols.description: Assure that `size(knotArray)` is `1 + degree + nControlPoints`

symbols.name: BSplineCurve
symbols.description:
The definition of a spline in 3D or 2D world space, or unitless 2D parameter space. 
 See also: `bSplineCurve(map)`

subfields.name: degree
subfields.description: The degree of the spline.

subfields.name: dimension
subfields.description: The dimension of the spline. Must be 2 or 3.

subfields.name: isRational
subfields.description: Whether the spline is rational.

subfields.name: isPeriodic
subfields.description: Whether the spline is periodic.

subfields.name: controlPoints
subfields.description: An array of control points of the required dimension. Size should be at least degree + 1. 2D spline control points can have world space units, or be unitless if they are intended to represent locations in parameter space (e.g. as a boundary input to `opCreateBSplineSurface`).

subfields.name: weights
subfields.description: Required if `isRational` is `true`An array of unitless values with the same size as the control points array.

subfields.name: knots
subfields.description: An array of non-decreasing knots of size equal to `1 + degree + size(controlPoints)`

symbols.name: canBeBSplineCurve
symbols.description: Typecheck for `BSplineCurve`

symbols.name: bSplineCurve

subfields.name: degree
subfields.description: The degree of the spline.

subfields.name: isPeriodic
subfields.description: Whether the spline is periodic.

subfields.name: controlPoints
subfields.description:
An array of control points. See `BSplineCurve` for specific detail. For periodic splines, you may provide the necessary overlap, or provide control points without any overlap. If no overlap is provided, `degree` overlapping control points (corresponding to the first `degree` control points) will be added to the end of the control points list (unless you provide a set of knots that show no overlap is necessary). 
 Example: 
`[vector(-1, 1, 0) * inch, vector(1, 1, 0) * inch, vector(1, -1, 0) * inch, vector(-1, -1, 0) * inch]`

subfields.name: weights
subfields.description: OptionalAn array of weights. See `BSplineCurve` for specific detail.

subfields.name: knots
subfields.description: OptionalAn array of knots. See `BSplineCurve` for specific detail. If knots are not provided a uniform parameterization wil be created such that the curve exists on the parameter range `[0, 1]`. For non-periodic curves with `n` control points, you may provide the full set of `n + degree + 1` knots, or you may provide `n - degree + 1` knots, and multiplicity will by padded onto the ends (which has the effect of clamping the spline to its two endpoints). For periodic curves with `n` unique control points (and optionally an additional `degree` overlapping control points), you may provide the full set of `n + 2 * degree + 1` knots, or you may provide `n + 1` knots, and the periodic knots will be padded onto the ends.

symbols.name: bSplineCurve
symbols.signature: (degree is number, isPeriodic is boolean, controlPoints is array, knots is KnotArray)
symbols.description: Deprecated: Use `bSplineCurve(map)`

symbols.name: bSplineCurve
symbols.signature: (degree is number, isPeriodic is boolean, controlPoints is array, weights is array, knots is KnotArray)
symbols.description: Deprecated: Use `bSplineCurve(map)`

math.fs.name: math
math.fs.description: A module containing many elementary math functions.Some math functions (such as `sin` and `cos`) accept a `ValueWithUnits`, rather than a number, and are defined in the units module. There is no `pow` function: exponentiation is done using the `^` operator.When writing a FeatureScript module which uses only basic math functionality, importing `mathUtils` (which imports this module along with `matrix`, `transform`, and `vector`) is recommended.

symbols.name: PI
symbols.description:
The mathematical constant pi, to floating-point precision.
 Example: 
`myAngle = (PI / 4) * radian`In most cases, conversions using `PI` can be avoided if using `ValueWithUnits` appropriately. Thus, you should never find yourself writing a statement like `sin(myAngle * PI / 180)`, since `myAngle` should already have correct units attached.

symbols.name: tolerantEquals
symbols.description:
Returns `true` if numbers are equal up to a computational tolerance. The tolerance used is a specific number defined by `TOLERANCE.computational` (set to `1e-13`) that is meant to adequately handle tolerances introduced by numerical operations. However, this tolerance may be too restrictive or generous for different situations, in which case `tolerantEquals(number, number, number)` should be used with the appropriate tolerance specified.
 Example: 
`tolerantEquals(1, 1) returns true` Example: 
`tolerantEquals(1, 1 + 1e-14) returns true` Example: 
`tolerantEquals(1, 1 / 900 * 9e100 / 1e98) returns true` Example: 
`tolerantEquals(1, 1 / 9 / 9 / 9 / 9 * 9 * 9 * 9 * 9) returns true` See also: `tolerantEquals(number, number, number)`

symbols.name: tolerantEquals
symbols.signature: (value1 is number, value2 is number, tolerance is number)
symbols.description:
Returns `true` if numbers are equal up to a specified tolerance.
 Example: 
`tolerantEquals(1, 1, 0) returns true` Example: 
`tolerantEquals(1, 1.01, 0.03) returns true` Example: 
`tolerantEquals(1, 0.99, 0.03) returns true` Example: 
`tolerantEquals(1, 1.01, 0.01) returns true` Example: 
`tolerantEquals(1, 1.03, 0.01) returns false` See also: `tolerantEquals(number, number)`

symbols.name: abs
symbols.description:
Absolute value.
 Example: 
`abs(-1) returns 1` Example: 
`abs(-1.5 * inch) equals 1.5 * inch`

symbols.name: sqrt
symbols.description:
Square root of a `number`.
 Example: 
`sqrt(4) returns 2` Example: 
`sqrt(-4) throws an error`

symbols.name: log
symbols.description:
Natural logarithm of a `number`.
 Example: 
`log(exp(3)) returns 3` Example: 
`log(0) returns -inf` Example: 
`log(-1) throws an error`

symbols.name: log10
symbols.description:
Base 10 logarithm of a `number`.
 Example: 
`log10(1000) returns 3` Example: 
`log10(0) returns -inf` Example: 
`log10(-1) throws an error`

symbols.name: sinh
symbols.description: Hyperbolic sine.

symbols.name: cosh
symbols.description: Hyperbolic cosine.

symbols.name: tanh
symbols.description: Hyperbolic tangent.

symbols.name: asinh
symbols.description: Inverse hyperbolic sine.

symbols.name: acosh
symbols.description: Inverse hyperbolic cosine.

symbols.name: atanh
symbols.description: Inverse hyperbolic tangent.

symbols.name: exp
symbols.description:
`e` to the power of `value`.
 Example: 
`exp(1) returns 2.71828...` Example: 
`exp(log(3)) returns 3`

symbols.name: exp2
symbols.description:
`2` to the power of `value`.
 Example: 
`exp2(10) returns 1024`

symbols.name: hypot
symbols.description:
Hypotenuse function, as `sqrt(a^2 + b^2)`, but without any surprising results due to finite numeric precision.
 Example: 
`hypot(3, 4) returns 5`

symbols.name: floor
symbols.description:
Round a `number` down to the nearest integer.
For values with units, first divide by a value with the same units.
 Example: 
`floor(1.9) returns 1` Example: 
`floor(2.0) returns 2` Example: 
`floor(-3.3) returns -4` Example: 
`var numberOfBricks is number = floor(wallLength / brickLength);`

symbols.name: ceil
symbols.description:
Round a `number` up to the nearest integer.
For values with units, first divide by a value with the same units.
 Example: 
`ceil(1.1) returns 2` Example: 
`ceil(1.0) returns 1` Example: 
`ceil(-3.3) returns -3` Example: 
`var numberOfBricks is number = ceil(wallLength / brickLength)`

symbols.name: round
symbols.description:
Round a `number` to the nearest integer.
 Example: 
`round(1.4) returns 1` Example: 
`round(1.5) returns 2` Example: 
`round(-1.5) returns -1`

symbols.name: roundToPrecision
symbols.description:
Round a `number` to a given number of decimal places.
 Example: 
`roundToPrecision(0.12345, 3) returns 0.123` Example: 
`roundToPrecision(9.9995, 3) returns 10` Example: 
`roundToPrecision(123.45, -1) returns 120`For positive values of precision, this method is more accurate than round(value, multiple). For instance, `print(roundToPrecision(0.45682, 4))` prints `0.4568`, but `round(0.45682, 0.0001)` prints `0.45680000000000004`. This is because the floating point representation of `0.0001` is slightly imprecise, and that imprecision is compounded inside the call to `round`. The floating point value of `4`, on the other hand, is precise, so the result of `roundToPrecision` will be the closest possible floating-point representation of `0.4568`. Thus, `print` and other functions using string conversion (`~`) will not print extraneous digits.

symbols.name: min
symbols.description:
Return the lesser of two values, which must be comparable with `<`.
 Example: 
`min(0, 1) returns 0` Example: 
`min(1 * meter, 1 * inch) equals 1 * inch`

symbols.name: max
symbols.description:
Return the greater of two values, which must be comparable with `<`. 
 Example: 
`max(0, 1) returns 1` Example: 
`max(1 * meter, 1 * inch) equals 1 * meter`

symbols.name: min
symbols.description:
Return the least of an array of values, as determined by operator `<`, or undefined if the array is empty.
 Example: 
`min([1, 2, 3]) returns 1` Example: 
`min([1 * inch, 2 * inch, 3 * inch]) equals 1 * inch`

symbols.name: max
symbols.description:
Return the greatest of an array of values, as determined by operator `<`, or undefined if the array is empty.
 Example: 
`max([1, 2, 3]) returns 3` Example: 
`max([1 * inch, 2 * inch, 3 * inch]) equals 3 * inch`

symbols.name: argMin
symbols.description:
Return the index of the smallest element of an array, as determined by operator `<`, or undefined if the array is empty.
 Example: 
`argMin([1 * inch, 2 * inch, 3 * inch]) returns 0`

symbols.name: argMax
symbols.description:
Return the index of the largest element of an array, as determined by the `>` operator, or undefined if the array is empty.
 Example: 
`argMax([1 * inch, 2 * inch, 3 * inch]) returns 2`

symbols.name: range
symbols.description:
Return an array of numbers in a range. Only integers are allowed. 
 Example: 
`range(0, 3) returns [0, 1, 2, 3]`

symbols.name: range
symbols.description:
Return an array of numbers, (of type `number` or `ValueWithUnits`), in a range. Note: before FeatureScript 372 this function received as input the step size instead of the number of steps
 Example: 
`range(0, 10, 6) returns [0, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10]` Example: 
`range(0, 4.5, 4) returns [0, 1.5, 3, 4.5]` Example: 
`range(1 * inch, 1.4 * inch, 3) returns [1 * inch, 1.2 * inch, 1.4 * inch]`

symbols.name: clamp
symbols.description:
Force a value into a range, 
 Example: 
`clamp(-1, 0, 20) returns 0,` Example: 
`clamp(10, 0, 20) returns 10` Example: 
`clamp(30, 0, 20) returns 20` Example: 
`clamp(30 * inch, 0 * inch, 20 * inch) equals 20 * inch`

symbols.name: isInteger
symbols.description:
True if `value` is a finite integer.
Note that all numbers in FeatureScript represented as floating point numbers, so an expression like `isInteger(hugeInteger + 0.1)` may still return `true`.
Used in feature preconditions to define an integer input.

symbols.name: isNonNegativeInteger
symbols.description: True if `value` is a finite integer greater than or equal to zero.

symbols.name: isPositiveInteger
symbols.description: True if `value` is a finite integer greater than zero.

mathUtils.fs.name: mathUtils
mathUtils.fs.description: This module imports the `math`, `matrix`, `transform`, and `vector` modules. It is designed to be imported instead of the `geometry` module in Feature Studios where only math (not higher-level modeling functionality) is needed.

symbols.name: Matrix
symbols.description: A `Matrix` is an array of rows, all the same size, each of which is an array of numbers

symbols.name: canBeMatrix
symbols.description: Typecheck for `Matrix`

symbols.name: matrixSize
symbols.description: Return a 2 element array containing the numbers of rows and columns of a matrix.

symbols.name: isSquare
symbols.description: Check whether a matrix is square.

symbols.name: matrix
symbols.description: Cast a two-dimensional array to a matrix.

symbols.name: identityMatrix
symbols.description: Construct an identity matrix of a given dimension.

symbols.name: zeroMatrix
symbols.description: Construct an all-zero matrix of a given dimension.

symbols.name: diagonalMatrix
symbols.description: Given an array of `diagonalValues` of size `n`, construct an `n`x`n` matrix which has those values along its main diagonal (starting in the top-left), and `0` everywhere else.

symbols.name: cwiseProduct
symbols.description: Construct a matrix by multiplying corresponding elements of two matrices (which must be the same size).

symbols.name: transpose
symbols.description: Return the transpose of a matrix.

symbols.name: inverse
symbols.description: Compute the inverse of a matrix. Throws an exception if the matrix is not square. If the matrix is singular the resulting matrix will contain infinities.

symbols.name: squaredNorm
symbols.description: Return the sum of the squares of matrix elements.

symbols.name: norm
symbols.description: Return the square root of the sum of the squares of matrix elements.

symbols.name: svd
symbols.description: Compute the singular value decomposition of a matrix, i.e. `s`, `u`, and `v`, where `m == u * s * transpose(v)` and s is a diagonal matrix of singular values.

parameters.name: m
parameters.description: an n-by-p matrix.

subfields.name: u
subfields.description: An n-by-n unitary matrix

subfields.name: s
subfields.description: An n-by-p diagonal matrix

subfields.name: v
subfields.description: A p-by-p unitary matrix

symbols.name: determinant
symbols.description: Return the determinant of the matrix.

symbols.name: MatrixWithUnits
symbols.description: A `MatrixWithUnits` is analogous to `ValueWithUnits`, but wrapping a matrix.

symbols.name: canBeMatrixWithUnits
symbols.description: Typecheck for `MatrixWithUnits`

symbols.name: get
symbols.signature: (matrix is MatrixWithUnits, i is number, j is number)
symbols.description: Gets an element of a MatrixWithUnits, returning a ValueWithUnits.

symbols.name: matrixSize
symbols.description: Return a 2-element array containing the numbers of rows and columns of a matrix.

symbols.name: removeKnots
symbols.signature: (points is array, knots is array, curveDegree is number)
symbols.description: Remove as many knots as possible from a NURBS defined by points, knots and curveDegree.

symbols.name: separatePointsAndWeights
symbols.description: Separates a 4d array containing weighted points into a 3d unweighted points array and a 1d weights array.

symbols.name: combinePointsAndWeights
symbols.description: Combines a 3d unweighted points array and a 1d weights array into a 4d weighted points array

symbols.name: PersistentCoordSystem
symbols.description:
A coordinate system that can persist as part of an attribute associated with an entity. This coordinate system will be transformed along with its parent entity as that entity undergoes transformations.
As with other attributes, the coordinate system will be propagated to copied entities, such as instances in a pattern. These copied persistent coordinate systems will take on the transforms of their new parents.
When `getAttribute` is used to retrieve a previously-set persistent coordinate system, the value of coordSystem will be in its transformed state for the current point in the feature execution. If a transform is applied such that the coordinate system is know longer right-handed, then the coordSystem value will be undefined. For instance, this would happen in the case of a mirrored coordinate system.

subfields.name: coordSystem
subfields.description: The coordinate system to persist

subfields.name: coordSystemId
subfields.description: An id to associate with the coordinate system. This id must be unique within the context of the parent entity.

subfields.name: forceRightHanded
subfields.description: When true, ensures the coordinate system remains right-handed when mirrored by inverting the Y-axis. Otherwise, mirroring the persistent coordinate system destroys it.

symbols.name: persistentCoordSystem
symbols.signature: (coordSystem is CoordSystem, coordSystemId is string, forceRightHanded is boolean)
symbols.description:
Creates a persistent coordinate system.
 See also: `PersistentCoordSystem`

parameters.name: coordSystem
parameters.description: The coordinate system

parameters.name: coordSystemId
parameters.description: An id with which to associate the coordinate system. This id must be unique within the context of the parent entity that an becomes associated with this persistent coordinate system through `setAttribute`.

parameters.name: forceRightHanded
parameters.description: When true, ensures the coordinate system remains right-handed when mirrored by inverting the Y-axis.

symbols.name: approximationTarget

symbols.name: approximateSpline
symbols.description: Compute a family of splines that approximates a family of `ApproximationTarget`s to within a given tolerance. The resulting splines are consistently parameterized, so that, for example, lofting between them will match corresponding target positions. Note: If `parameters` are not specified, the magnitude of start and end derivatives in targets is ignored as well as the second component parallel with the first derivative.

subfields.name: degree
subfields.description: The desired degree of the curve. The output may have a different degree, if for example there aren't enough points.

subfields.name: tolerance
subfields.description: How far the output is allowed to deviate from the input. Must be at least 1e-8 meters.

subfields.name: isPeriodic
subfields.description: Whether the output spline is periodic.

subfields.name: targets
subfields.description: An array of `ApproximationTarget`s. All targets must have the same number of positions and specify corresponding derivative information.

subfields.name: parameters
subfields.description: OptionalAn array of numbers representing the parameters corresponding to the target points. Must be strictly increasing. If specified, the output spline at those parameters will match the target points. If specified, derivatives in approximation targets will not be rescaled.

subfields.name: maxControlPoints
subfields.description: OptionalThe maximum number of control points that will be returned by this function's output. Tolerance will not be satisfied if this limit is reached. Default is 10000.

subfields.name: interpolateIndices
subfields.description: OptionalAn array of indices into target positions that specifies which ones are to be interpolated exactly. This is currently supported only for non-periodic splines.

subfields.name: suppressInterpolationNotice
subfields.description: OptionalDon't report an info if the result is fully interpolated. Default is false.

returnInfo.description: An array of `BSplineCurve`s, one for each target.

subfields.name: controlPoints
subfields.description:
A matrix of control points. See `BSplineSurface` for specific detail. If u or v is periodic, you may provide the necessary overlap, or provide control points without any overlap. If no overlap is provided, `degree` overlapping control point rows or columns (corresponding to the first `degree` control point rows or columns) will be added. (unless you provide a set of knots that show no overlap is necessary). 
 Example: 

```
controlPointMatrix([
    [vector(-1, -1, -1) * inch, vector(-1, 0, 0) * inch, vector(-1, -2, 1) * inch],
    [vector( 0,  1, -1) * inch, vector( 0, 2, 0) * inch, vector( 0,  0, 1) * inch],
    [vector( 1, -1, -1) * inch, vector( 1, 0, 0) * inch, vector( 1, -2, 1) * inch]
])
```

subfields.name: weights
subfields.description: OptionalA matrix of weights. See `BSplineSurface` for specific detail.

subfields.name: uKnots
subfields.description: OptionalAn array of knots. See `BSplineSurface` for specific detail. If knots are not provided a uniform parameterization will be created such that the u parameterization exists on the range `[0, 1]`. For non-periodic u with `n` control point rows, you may provide the full set of `n + degree + 1` knots, or you may provide `n - degree + 1` knots, and multiplicity will by padded onto the ends (which has the effect of clamping the surface to the control points in the first and last rows). For periodic u with `n` unique control points (and optionally an additional `degree` overlapping control points), you may provide the full set of `n + 2 * degree + 1` knots, or you may provide `n + 1` knots, and the periodic knots will be padded onto the ends.

subfields.name: vKnots
subfields.description: OptionalSee `uKnots`.

symbols.name: Transform
symbols.description:
A `Transform` typically represents a change of position and orientation in 3D space (other affine transformations, such as scaling and shearing, can also be represented).
`rotationAround`, `scaleUniformly`, `transform(Vector)`, `toWorld(CoordSystem)` and `fromWorld(CoordSystem)` return useful transforms. `Transform`s are commonly used with `opTransform`, whose documentation has examples of calling these functions.
`Transform`s are also commonly used with their `*` operator overloads to easily work with geometry in multiple coordinate systems. 
 Example: 
`transform * (vector(1, 1, 1) * inch) yields a point which is the given point, transformed by the transform.` Example: 
`transform2 * transform1 yields a new transform which is equivalent to applying transform1 followed by transform2.`A `Transform` contains a linear portion (rotation, scaling, or shearing), which is applied first, and a translation vector, which is applied second. Generally, these individual fields on this type don't need to be directly used, and everything you need can be accomplished through the operator overloads above, or other functions in this module and the `coordSystem` module.

subfields.name: linear

symbols.name: ValueWithUnits
symbols.description:
A `ValueWithUnits` is a number with dimensions, such as 1.5 inches, 90 degrees, or 9.81 meters per second per second. 

```
const width is ValueWithUnits = 1.5 * inch;
const angle is ValueWithUnits = 90 * degree;
const g     is ValueWithUnits = 9.81 * meter / second / second;
```

Values with the same dimensions can be added and subtracted, even if they were created in different unit systems. 

```
const length       = 3 * meter + 1 * inch;
const longerLength = length + 0.01 * inch;
const nonsense     = 3 * meter + 3 * degree;     // Throws an error from dimension mismatch
```

Multiplication (`*`) will multiply both the values and the units. An expression where the units all cancel evaluates to plain `number`. 

```
var doubleLength   = 2 * length;                 // ValueWithUnits with length units
var area           = (20 * foot) * (30 * foot);  // ValueWithUnits with area units
var numberOfBricks = (64 * foot) / (9 * inch);   // number with no units
```

Values with units can be raised to numerical powers with the `^` operator. Base units like `inch` or `second` can be exponentiated in the same way. 

```
var squareArea   = (3 * meter)^2;
var g            = 9.81 * meter / second^2;
```

Functions in the standard library require a ValueWithUnits for arguments where units are needed. Thus, the `depth` in `opExtrude` requires a value with length units (rather than assuming meters). The argument of `sin` is a value with angle units (rather than assuming radians). The argument of sqrt can be any value whose units are even powers. 

```
var ladderHeight   = ladderLength * sin(75 * degree); // Has length units
var pendulumPeriod = 2 * PI * sqrt(armLength / g);    // Has time units
```

Equality of `ValueWithUnits` considers the underlying value, so `25.4 * millimeter` is equal to `1 * inch`. However, `PI * radian / 5` does not equal `36 * degree` because of finite precision arithmetic. To check equality of `ValueWithUnits`, you should use tolerantEquals. 

```
if (tolerantEquals(myLength, 0 * inch))
{
    ...
```

Keeping correct units on variables is always best practice, in order to benefit from easy unit conversions and runtime unit checks. However, when printing, you may wish to divide out the units in order to display a value in a different system of units. 

```
const length = 42 * centimeter;
println(length);                                 // prints "0.42 meter"
println("length: " ~ toString(length));          // prints "length: 0.42 meter"
println(length / inch ~ " inches");              // prints "16.535433070866137 inches"
println(roundToPrecision(length / inch, 3) ~ " inches"); // prints "16.535 inches"
```

symbols.name: tolerantEquals
symbols.description: Returns true if angles are equal up to zeroAngle or anything else is equal up to zeroLength

symbols.name: tolerantGreaterThan
symbols.description:
Returns `true` if `greater` is greater than and not tolerantly equal to `lesser`.
 Example: 
`(1 * meter)->tolerantGreaterThan(0 * meter) returns true` Example: 
`1->tolerantGreaterThan(0) returns true` Example: 
`0->tolerantGreaterThan(-1e-14) returns false` Example: 
`0->tolerantGreaterThan(0) returns false`

symbols.name: tolerantGreaterThanOrEqual
symbols.description:
Returns `true` if `greater` is greater than or tolerantly equal to `lesser`.
 Example: 
`(1 * meter)->tolerantGreaterThanOrEqual(0 * meter) returns true` Example: 
`1->tolerantGreaterThanOrEqual(0) returns true` Example: 
`0->tolerantGreaterThanOrEqual(0) returns true` Example: 
`0->tolerantGreaterThanOrEqual(1e-14) returns true`

symbols.name: tolerantGreaterThanOrEqual
symbols.signature: (greater is number, lesser is number, tolerance is number)

symbols.name: tolerantLessThan
symbols.description:
Returns `true` if `lesser` is less than and not tolerantly equal to `greater`.
 Example: 
`(0 * meter)->tolerantLessThan(1 * meter) returns true` Example: 
`0->tolerantLessThan(1) returns true` Example: 
`0->tolerantLessThan(1e-14) returns false` Example: 
`0->tolerantLessThan(0) returns false`

symbols.name: tolerantLessThanOrEqual
symbols.description:
Returns `true` if `lesser` is less than or tolerantly equal to `greater`.
 Example: 
`(0 * meter)->tolerantLessThanOrEqual(1 * meter) returns true` Example: 
`0->tolerantLessThanOrEqual(1) returns true` Example: 
`0->tolerantLessThanOrEqual(0) returns true` Example: 
`1e-14->tolerantLessThanOrEqual(0) returns true`

symbols.name: tolerantWithinExclusive
symbols.description:
Returns `true` if `value` is tolerantly within the interval `(lesser, greater)`.
 Example: 
`(0.5 * meter)->tolerantWithinExclusive(0 * meter, 1 * meter) returns true` Example: 
`0.5->tolerantWithinExclusive(0, 1) returns true` Example: 
`1->tolerantWithinExclusive(0, 1) returns false` Example: 
`0->tolerantWithinExclusive(0, 1) returns false` Example: 
`0->tolerantWithinExclusive(1e-14, 1) returns false`

symbols.name: tolerantWithinInclusive
symbols.description:
Returns `true` if `value` is tolerantly within the interval `[lesser, greater]`.
 Example: 
`(0.5 * meter)->tolerantWithinInclusive(0 * meter, 1 * meter) returns true` Example: 
`0.5->tolerantWithinInclusive(0, 1) returns true` Example: 
`1->tolerantWithinInclusive(0, 1) returns true` Example: 
`0->tolerantWithinInclusive(0, 1) returns true` Example: 
`0->tolerantWithinInclusive(1e-14, 1) returns true`

symbols.name: tolerantEqualsZero
symbols.description:
Returns `true` if `value` is tolerantly equal to the `0` value with the same units as `value`.
 Example: 
`tolerantEqualsZero(0) returns true` Example: 
`tolerantEqualsZero(0 * meter) returns true` Example: 
`tolerantEqualsZero(1e-9 * meter) returns true` Example: 
`tolerantEqualsZero(1) returns false` Example: 
`tolerantEqualsZero(1 * meter) returns false`

symbols.name: sqrt
symbols.description:
Square root of a `ValueWithUnits`.
 Example: 
`sqrt(4 * meter^2) equals 2 * meter.` Example: 
`2 * PI * sqrt(armLength / (9.8 * meter/second^2)) equals the period of a pendulum, in seconds.` Example: 
`sqrt(4 * meter) throws an error, since FeatureScript has no concept of the square root of a meter.`

symbols.name: hypot
symbols.description:
Hypotenuse function, as `sqrt(a^2 + b^2)`, but without any surprising results due to finite numeric precision.
 Example: 
`hypot(3 * foot, 4 * foot) equals 5 * foot`

symbols.name: sin
symbols.description:
Sine, the ratio of the opposite side over the hypotenuse in a right triangle of the specified angle.
 Example: 
`sin(30 * degree) returns approximately 0.5` Example: 
`sin(PI * radian) returns approximately 0`

symbols.name: cos
symbols.description:
Cosine, the ratio of the adjacent side over the hypotenuse in a right triangle of the specified angle.
 Example: 
`cos(60 * degree) returns approximately 0.5` Example: 
`cos(PI * radian) returns approximately -1`

symbols.name: tan
symbols.description:
Tangent, the ratio of the opposite side over the adjacent side in a right triangle of the specified angle.
 Example: 
`tan(45 * degree) returns approximately 1` Example: 
`tan(PI * radian) returns approximately 0`

symbols.name: asin
symbols.description:
Arcsine, i.e. inverse sine.
Returns a value between `-90 * degree` and `90 * degree`.
 Example: 
`asin(0.5) equals approximately 30 * degree` Example: 
`asin(1.5) throws an error, since there is no value where sin(value) is 1.5`

symbols.name: acos
symbols.description:
Arccosine, i.e. inverse cosine.
Returns a value between `0 * degree` and `180 * degree`.
 Example: 
`acos(0.5) equals approximately 60 * degree` Example: 
`acos(1.5) throws an error, since there is no value where cos(value) is 1.5`

symbols.name: atan
symbols.description:
Arctangent, i.e. inverse tangent.
Returns a value between `-90 * degree` and `90 * degree`.
 Example: 
`atan(1) equals approximately 45 * degree` Example: 
`atan(inf) equals approximately 90 * degree`

symbols.name: atan2
symbols.description:
Returns the counterclockwise angle from the vector `[0, 1]` to the vector `[x, y]`. The angle is negative if y is negative. This is equivalent to `atan(y/x)` except the result respects the quadrant of the input and is well-behaved near x == 0.
 Example: 
`atan2(0, 1) equals approximately 0 * degree` Example: 
`atan2(1, 0) equals approximately 90 * degree` Example: 
`atan2(0, -1) equals approximately 180 * degree` Example: 
`atan2(-1, 0) equals approximately -90 * degree`

symbols.name: atan2
symbols.description:
Returns the counterclockwise angle from the vector `[0, 1]` to the vector `[x, y]`, assuming the units of `y` and `x` match.
 See also: `atan2(number, number)`

symbols.name: isAngleBetween
symbols.signature: (queryAngle is ValueWithUnits, minAngle is ValueWithUnits, maxAngle is ValueWithUnits)
symbols.description:
Returns true if the provided angle is within the given range (inclusive with tolerance), "winding" the query angle as necessary to put it within a positive full circle turn of the range. Ranges that encompass one or more full circles will return true regardless of the query angle.
Throws if range's maximum angle is less than the minimum angle.
 Example: 
`isAngleBetween(0.5 * PI * radian, 0 * radian, PI * radian) returns true` Example: 
`isAngleBetween(0.5 * PI * radian, 2 * PI * radian, 3 * PI * radian) returns true` Example: 
`isAngleBetween(-1.5 * PI * radian, 0 * radian, PI * radian) returns true`

symbols.name: floor
symbols.description:
Round a value down to nearest given multiple.
 Example: 
`floor(125, 10) returns 120` Example: 
`floor(-15, 10) returns -20` Example: 
`floor(3.14 * inch, 0.1 * inch) equals 3.1 * inch`

symbols.name: ceil
symbols.description:
Round a value up to nearest given multiple.
 Example: 
`ceil(125, 10) returns 130` Example: 
`ceil(-15, 10) returns -10` Example: 
`ceil(3.14 * inch, 0.1 * inch) equals 3.2 * inch`

symbols.name: round
symbols.description:
Round a value to nearest given multiple.
 Example: 
`round(125, 10) returns 130` Example: 
`round(-15, 10) returns -10` Example: 
`round((10 / 3) * meter, centimeter) equals 3.33 * meter` Example: 
`round(1 * meter, .001 * inch) equals 39.37 * inch`For small values of `multiple`, `roundToPrecision` is preferred to reduce floating point errors.

symbols.name: toString
symbols.description: General value to string conversion.

symbols.name: parseJsonWithUnits
symbols.description: Parse a JSON string into either a map or array. Null values in the JSON are returned as `undefined`. Throws if the string is not well-formed JSON. Applicable strings are parsed into a ValueWithUnits. For instance, "3 inch" will map to a `ValueWithUnits` with length units that repreresents 3 inches.

returnInfo.description: A map or an array corresponding to the JSON value.

symbols.name: Vector
symbols.description:
A `Vector` is a non-empty array. It should contain numbers or lengths.
Operators `+`, `-`, `*`, and `/` are overloaded for vectors, and other operations such as dot product are available. If a vector does not contain numbers or lengths, operations that assume number-like properties may fail.

symbols.name: canBeVector
symbols.description: Typecheck for `Vector`

symbols.name: vector
symbols.description: Make a Vector from an array.

symbols.name: vector
symbols.description: Construct a 2-dimensional vector.

symbols.name: vector
symbols.description: Construct a 3-dimensional vector.

symbols.name: isLengthVector
symbols.description:
True for a `Vector` where all members are values with length units. 
 Example: 
`vector([1, 2, 3, 4, 5]) * inch`

symbols.name: isUnitlessVector
symbols.description:
True for a `Vector` where all members are simple `number`s. 
 Example: 
`vector([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])`

symbols.name: is2dPoint
symbols.description:
True for a single 2D length `Vector` 
 Example: 
`vector(0.5, 1) * inch`

symbols.name: is2dPointVector
symbols.description:
True for an `array` where all members are 2D lengths. 
 Example: 
`[vector(0, 0) * inch, vector(0, 1) * inch, vector(1, 0) * inch]`

symbols.name: is2dDirection
symbols.description:
True for a unitless 2D `Vector` that is normalized (i.e. has length `1`) 
 Example: 
`vector(0, 1)`

symbols.name: is3dLengthVector
symbols.description:
True for a 3D `Vector` where all members are values with length units. 
 Example: 
`vector(0, 1.5, 30) * inch`

symbols.name: is3dDirection
symbols.description:
True for a unitless 3D `Vector` that is normalized (i.e. has length `1`) 
 Example: 
`vector(0, 0, 1)`

symbols.name: zeroVector
symbols.description:
Make an array filled with 0. 
 Example: 
`zeroVector(3) is equivalent to vector(0, 0, 0)`

symbols.name: squaredNorm
symbols.description: Returns the squared length of a vector. This is slightly faster to calculate than the length.

symbols.name: norm
symbols.description: Returns the length (norm) of a vector.

symbols.name: dot
symbols.description: Returns the dot product of two vectors.

symbols.name: cross
symbols.description: Returns the cross product of two 3-dimensional vectors.

symbols.name: angleBetween
symbols.description:
Returns the angle between two 3-dimensional vectors. Values are within the range `[0, PI] * radian`. 
 Example: 
`angleBetween(X_DIRECTION, Y_DIRECTION) equals PI/2 * radian` Example: 
`angleBetween(Y_DIRECTION, X_DIRECTION) equals PI/2 * radian`A plane is fitted to the two vectors and the shortest angle between them is measured on that plane.

symbols.name: angleBetween
symbols.signature: (vector1 is Vector, vector2 is Vector, ref is Vector)
symbols.description:
Returns the counterclockwise angle between two 3-dimensional vectors as witnessed from the tip of a third 3-dimensional vector. Values are within the range `(-PI, PI] * radian` with negative values indicating clockwise angles. 
 Example: 
`angleBetween(X_DIRECTION, Y_DIRECTION, Z_DIRECTION) equals PI/2 * radian` Example: 
`angleBetween(Y_DIRECTION, X_DIRECTION, Z_DIRECTION) equals -PI/2 * radian`The first two vectors are projected onto a plane perpendicular to the reference vector and the angle is measured according to that projection.

symbols.name: normalize
symbols.description: Returns the (unitless) result of normalizing vector. Throws if the input is zero-length.

parameters.name: vector
parameters.description: A Vector with any units.

symbols.name: project
symbols.description: Project the `source` vector onto the `target` vector. Equivalent to `target * dot(source, target) / squaredNorm(target)`.

symbols.name: perpendicularVector
symbols.description: Returns a vector perpendicular to the given vector. The choice of which perpendicular vector to return is arbitrary but consistent for the same input. The returned vector is unitless and of length 1.

symbols.name: rotationMatrix3d
symbols.description: Construct a 3D rotation matrix that represents the minimum rotation that takes the normalized `from` vector to the normalized `to` vector. The inputs may have any units.

symbols.name: rotationMatrix3d
symbols.description: Construct a 3D matrix representing a counterclockwise (looking against the axis) rotation around the given axis by the given rotation angle.

symbols.name: scalarTripleProduct
symbols.signature: (vector1 is Vector, vector2 is Vector, vector3 is Vector)
symbols.description: Returns the scalar triple product, a dot (b cross c), of three 3-dimensional vectors.

symbols.name: tolerantEquals
symbols.description: Returns true if two vectors designate the same point (within tolerance) or the same direction (within tolerance).

symbols.name: parallelVectors
symbols.description: Returns true if two vectors are parallel (within tolerance).

symbols.name: perpendicularVectors
symbols.description: Returns true if two vectors are perpendicular (within tolerance).

symbols.name: clusterPoints
symbols.description: Groups points into clusters. Two points farther than tolerance apart are guaranteed to be in separate clusters. A set of points all within tolerance of each other that has no other points within tolerance is guaranteed to be a single cluster.

returnInfo.description: Array of arrays, where each array is a cluster of nearby points, represented as indices into points array.
